clean_story:
Earth’s restless tremors. Energy hidden in motion.

The term seismic power harvesting means capturing the kinetic energy from earthquakes or vibrations—like a windmill, but for the planet’s shudders instead of breezes.

The challenge isn’t just catching the energy; it’s doing so without breaking the harvester first. Earthquakes release energy in unpredictable bursts—a magnitude 6 quake unleashes as much power as 6,000 tons of TNT, but in erratic waves. Most harvesters use electromagnetic induction (a magnet moving through a coil), yet even reinforced designs struggle: Japan’s 2011 quake generated vibrations strong enough to snap steel beams like twigs. Smaller-scale tests, like a 2020 prototype in Italy, extracted just 0.1 watts per tremor—barely enough to power an LED. The real hurdle? Efficiency drops 90% if the device isn’t tuned to the exact frequency of the quake, like a guitar string failing to resonate with the wrong pluck.

Three related subjects are vibration energy scavengers, piezoelectric roadways, and geothermal power trade-offs.
